infons.title: Keywords

passages.text: Automated knee MRI segmentation with KneeSeg-U for cartilage and bone extraction using an unsupervised deep learning framework

The segmentation of knee cartilage and bone in magnetic resonance images with supervised learning methods needs manual annotations from experts, although it demands high amounts of labeled data. KneeSeg-U presents itself as a deep learning unsupervised framework that extracts cartilage segments and bone structures from knee MR imaging automatically with unmarked scan data. KneeSeg-U provides automated full-system segmentation with precise results through unaided clinical efficacy and by negating the requirement for expert annotations. The creation of our extensive knee MRI dataset involved obtaining various sequences from both open-access repositories and clinical points of origin. During training, we applied domain-specific enhancement approaches along with contrastive learning algorithms to build better generalization abilities. The U-Net architecture applied in KneeSeg-U remains trainable through adversarial methods that incorporate self-supervised learning features with consistency restrictions for achieving precise segmentations. Experimental tests demonstrate KneeSeg-U produces segmentations that match supervised methods through a cartilage Dice similarity measure of 0.87, even as bone Dice similarity reaches 0.91 above typical unsupervised segmentation methodology. The system shows generalization functionality for different MRI protocols and multiple anatomical patterns. The KneeSeg-U framework represents an effective method to automate MRI knee segmentation without needing annotated references for building adaptable methods in clinical research about musculoskeletal disease diagnosis.
Introduction

Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) provides extensive clinical value for diagnosing musculoskeletal problems with its primary role in detecting osteoarthritis as well as other joint degeneration diseases. Segmentation of knee cartilage and bone in MRI scans must be accurately performed to support early diagnosis, together with disease monitoring and therapy development. Current cartilage and bone segmentation methods used by radiologists, along with automated supervised learning methods, face specific drawbacks.

Accurate segmentation of knee cartilage and bone in the MRI is traditionally achieved through expert manual annotation, which is very time-consuming and labor-intensive. Delineation of thin layers of cartilage as well as complicated bone-cartilage interfaces requires special radiological skill, making the procedure unsuitable for large-scale studies or routine clinical use. Moreover, manual segmentation is by nature a subjective process and hence, there is a tendency for inter-observer variability, which can impact reproducibility and quantitative measurements, such as cartilage thickness and volume. Supervised deep learning methods try to automate this task however they require large, high quality labelled data sets. The establishment of such data sets is expensive, taking a great deal of expert effort, standardized protocols, and quality control. These difficulties cause a lack of scaling and the widespread use of automated segmentation systems in various clinical settings. Consequently, there exists an urgent need for approaches on unsupervised learning that are able to make use of intrinsic image characteristics without the need to be annotated manually, and in this way reduce the dependency on expert labeling while keeping clinically meaningful segmentation performance.

Unsupervised methods employ self-supervisory and feature consistency learning paradigms to achieve segmentation instead of direct optimization with supervised datasets, while needing a precise design for producing meaningful clinical segmentations. The heterogeneity of knee MRI scans becomes substantial because of multiple acquisition setting types and scanner types, and individual anatomic variations. Different imaging conditions create challenges for general segmentation models because of which robust feature extraction techniques need to be developed for diverse imaging situations.

Traditional unsupervised segmentation methods, such as clustering-based approaches and intensity thresholding, are often inadequate for knee MRI due to their inability to capture complex anatomical structures and variations in tissue contrast. While recent self-supervised learning techniques have shown promise in medical image analysis, their application to knee MRI segmentation remains underexplored, particularly in terms of achieving high segmentation accuracy without external supervision. Adversarial training and contrastive learning have been successfully employed in other medical imaging tasks, but their integration into a unified framework for knee cartilage and bone segmentation poses unique methodological challenges. Ensuring that an unsupervised deep learning model can effectively leverage both local anatomical consistency and global structural relationships is essential for achieving clinically acceptable results. A comparison between present knee segmentation frameworks and KneeSeg-U appears in Table 1.

The inability of manual marking and the reliance on a large dataset of labeled images for the training of supervised learning models underscore the dire need for a powerful unsupervised segmentation model for knee MRI. An effective solution need to be able to learn intrinsic anatomical structures by itself without relying on expert-provided labels whilst at the same time being tolerant to variations in imaging protocol and patient-specific anatomy. In this context, the task of KneeSeg-U is to address these challenges using self-supervised representation learning, contrastive feature modeling and adversarial optimization in a modified U-Net architecture. With anatomical consistency constraints and protocol invariant feature learning effectively combined, the proposed framework is an effective way to obtain reliable segmentation of cartilages and bones, which does not require manual annotation. This approach not only minimizes the cost and labor that is associated with the curation of a dataset, but also offers a scalable and clinically practical solution to the problem of assessing and longitudinally tracking musculoskeletal diseases.
The key contributions of this work are as follows:

Architectural Novelty - novel design of a modified U-Net backbone slice combination of attention-guided skip connections, dilated residual bottlenecks and multi-scale feature extraction for anatomically coherent segmentation.

Training Strategy Innovation - novel unsupervised training strategy fully unsupervised end-to-end pipeline integrating contrastive feature learning, anatomically informed affinity propagation clustering and slice-wise temporal consistency to tackle the heterogeneity and anatomical variability of MRI.

Experimental Validation - extensive experimental evaluation using large scale knee MRI datasets with benchmark performance for anatomical segmentation was evaluated and enhanced participates to the state-of-the-art unsupervised learning method in this study.

The details of the study are presented in the subsequent parts as follows. Section  2 presents a review of the current studies on knee MRI segmentation and various deep learning methods. Section  3 of this study provides a detailed explanation of KneeSeg-U, the self-supervision approach, and training procedures. The conclusion and discussion section is further divided into Subsections to elaborate on more points on results and their comparisons with the baseline method and possible clinical application. Therefore, Sect.  5 provides a summary of the study along with the research implications and recommendations.
Related methods

The development of image analysis in medical imaging has been greatly influenced by deep learning especially in cases where limited annotations, data heterogeneity, and privacy requirements are imposed. Recent investigations have been able to explore self-supervised learning (SSL), unsupervised representation learning, federated learning and transformer-based architectures to mitigate these challenges while improving robustness and clinical applicability.

Self-supervised and unsupervised representation learning has become a hot topic to overcome the scarcity of annotation. Manna et al. propose a self-supervised framework to learn the anatomical representations of space based on the knee MRI video clips, showing a good performance in multilabel diagnosis of injury in class-imbalanced datasets without the help of class imbalance correction. Similarly, Felfeliyan et al. propose the self-supervised Mask-RCNN, which predicts and corrects the artificial distortions and it improved the accuracy of the segmentation using limited supervision to a high degree. Dissanayake et al. conducted a survey that comprehensively categorize the various methods for SSL and few-shot learning and are observed to be effective. It also reveals the continued generalization and deployment challenges of SSL across data sets and modalities. Conceptually oriented works, including Larin et al. and Getty et al. go further into unsupervised learning into non-Euclidean spaces and towards neuromorphic paradigms, although both are largely exploratory so far with little quantitative validation of them.

Federated and privacy-preserving learning has been gaining momentum because of the growing concerns about data sharing in healthcare. Ahmed et al., suggested a Federated Adversarial Learning for the Reconstruction of Medical Images. Levac et al. also investigate federated unrolled optimization models for MRI reconstruction and show the model can be robust to limited local data. Despite promising findings, both studies admit the presence of difficulties in terms of computation time, communication time and heterogeneity across clients, thus potentially hindering potential scalability under resource constrained settings, as in clinical questions.

Domain adaptation and label-efficient segmentation are other topics that are currently being investigated. Li et al. proposed a source-free Unsupervised Domain Adaptation for Knee MRI Segmentation via Uncertainty-aware Pseudo-Labeling. He et al. extend this idea by introducing a joint unsupervised and semi-supervised pipeline for performing multi-sequence abdominal MRI by taking anatomical constraints into account to make it robust. However, these multi-stage approaches are prone to propagation of mistakes and added pipeline complicate, raises the concern of stability in clinical data.

Architectural innovations in segmentation and reconstruction also have proven of critical importance. Transformer-based and hybrid CNN-transformer models have shown to have large performance gains compared to traditional CNNs as reviewed by Shah et al., particularly by capturing the long-range dependencies. Gai et al. and Woo et al. are dedicated to the problem of knee MRI segmentation and propose dual attention and anomaly-aware segmentation frameworks which can enhance the performance in pathological cases. However, many of these studies are based on selected data sets and are not cross-institutional validated. In parallel, Feng et al. and Pinaya et al. manage to explore unsupervised reconstruction and anomaly detection (via implicit neural representations and diffusion models, respectively) with a strong result and at the cost of high computational complexity and, issues of scalability.

Clinical relevance and efficiency considerations are covered in fewer works. von Schacky et al. depicts a multitask deep learning model for bone tumor analysis with expert-level performance, making the potential of integrated detection and segmentation systems as possible. Yusuf et al. point out lightweight and compressed models to be used in low-resource environments where some accuracy is sacrificed for the sake of accessibility. Comprehensive surveys by Mahendrakar et al., Patel et al. and Moujahid et al. (2024) further bring in the perspective of progress in knee imaging and multi-view learning while gaps in interpretability, reproducibility and standardised evaluation.Song et al. claim cluster-centered transformers enhance low-contrast plaque segmentation by the use of multi-granularity ecosystem: as the dependence on to clustering procedure for segmentation may reduce the adaptability according to segmentation to a expected ghostly dental valley or irregular noise. Guo et al. proposed a query-focused bidirectional Mamba improves few-shot segmentation with contextual modelling and adversarial learning, the increased complexity of its architecture may impact the stability of its training and efficiency of its computing operations.

Due to the recent development of self-supervised learning (SSL), MRI segmentation and reconstruction has seen a significant change, especially in applications with a limited amount of annotated data. Song et al. proposed about REHRSeg which is a framework for resource-efficient segmentation (and is efficient in terms of resource uses) than state-of-the-art segmentation resources for generating high-resolution (HR) segmentations from low-resolution (LR) MRI scans. Key innovations are, an uncertainty-aware super-resolution (UASR) head to handle the boundary ambiguities and a structural knowledge distillation to align super-resolution and segmentation feature to enhance the overall accuracy. Li et al. reviewed the SSL approaches for MRI reconstruction and highlighted that it overcomes the limitations of fully sampled k space requirements, reduces the scan times and retains the image quality along with the need for robust validation for anatomies. Zhang et al. showed the effectiveness of SSL in small numbers of samples for segmenting metastatic brain tumors with Swin UNETR and obtained high sensitivity even with 33 cases. Further, 3DINO is a generalizable 3D SSL framework pretrained on ~ 100,000 scans of different organs is offered by Xu et al. and led to better performance in downstream tasks. These studies have all shown the potential of using SSL in accurate, scalable and resource-efficient analysis of MRI data. Research gaps

Despite advancements in knee MRI segmentation, several limitations persist across existing methodologies. Supervised deep learning approaches, while achieving high accuracy, are heavily reliant on large, annotated datasets. The manual labeling process required for these datasets is labour-intensive, time-consuming, and prone to inter-observer variability, making it challenging to acquire sufficient high-quality labeled data for effective model training.

Unsupervised domain adaptation: Unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA) techniques aim to mitigate the scarcity of labeled data by adapting models trained on labeled source domains to unlabelled target domains. However, the performance of UDA methods is highly dependent on the similarity between source and target domains. Significant domain shifts, such as differences in MRI acquisition protocols or patient populations, can adversely affect the adaptation process, leading to suboptimal segmentation performance.

Self-supervised learning: Self-supervised learning (SSL) leverages unlabelled data through pretext tasks to learn useful representations, reducing the need for large labeled datasets. Nevertheless, the effectiveness of SSL is contingent upon the design of these pretext tasks and the quality of the unlabelled data. Inadequate or irrelevant pretext tasks may result in learned features that do not generalize well to the actual segmentation task, thereby limiting the model’s performance.

Semi-supervised learning: Semi-supervised learning methods combine labeled and unlabelled data to enhance segmentation accuracy. However, these approaches often require careful selection of representative labeled samples and may involve complex training procedures. Additionally, the presence of noisy or uninformative unlabeled data can negatively impact model performance, necessitating robust strategies to handle such data effectively.​.

Generative adversarial networks: GANs have been studied in the domain of medical image segmentation, especially in unsupervised and semi-supervised contexts, in which they are generally applied to modeling data distributions, increasing the realism of features, and domain adaptation instead of performing segmentation.

Conditional diffusion models: The unsupervised segmentation operation requires solutions from conditional diffusion models. These models need both substantial computational power in addition to strict adjustments of parameters for their usage. The models demonstrate performance changes according to data distribution complexity thus creating hurdles for their use in different clinical setups.

Adversarial domain adaptation techniques and mix-up methods: It improves the strength of MRI acquisition settings. A better performance result from these methods yet their deployment requires intensive process training and multiple dataset acquisition for proper functioning. The instability of these techniques when dealing with sparse data distribution prevents their wider implementation.

The present knee MRI segmentation approaches face multiple issues because they can operate only using particular datasets and need adaptable domains along with complex training protocols and massive computational power. A new framework requires development since it needs to produce precise segmentations regardless of datasets size or intricate training protocols.
Comparison with KneeSeg-U

KneeSeg-U is a completely unsupervised model that is not trained on annotated data. Rather, it is trained by unlabeled knee MRI scans through contrastive learning, adversarial training, and consistency. It allows representation of features automatically, segmentation without manual labels and produces a high level of generalization across MRI protocols. The proposed model utilizes contrastive learning techniques with domain-specific augmentations to achieve improved generalization among various MRI protocols since it operates differently from other sensitive unsupervised domain adaptation approaches. KneeSeg-U employs feature consistency constraints to execute automatic representation learning without the time-intensive task engineering those traditional self-supervised learning needs.

Users of the proposed method operate without labeled data because its learning mechanisms operate via unlabeled input images. Stable segmentation outputs result from the KneeSeg-U architecture based on the modified U-Net because it implements regulated adversarial loss control to stabilize GAN network operations. The KneeSeg-U model matches results from conditional diffusion models while using training strategies that require less processing power compared to their extensive demands. The robustness techniques mix-up and adversarial domain adaptation achieve good performance with multiple datasets; however, KneeSeg-U demonstrates excellent generalization capabilities because it functions independently with its own datasets. Experimental studies confirm that KneeSeg-U reaches better outcomes than typical unsupervised procedures. The method delivers equivalent outcome quality to supervised methods, which makes it suitable for large-scale knee MRI segmentation without needing manual annotations. Collectively, the literature shows that there has been much advancement in the fields of self-supervised, federated, and unsupervised medical image analysis, particularly with MRI-based tasks. However, there are still some gaps, such as cross-modality and cross-institutional validation, computational complexity that makes it difficult to deploy it in the real world, pseudo-labeling errors, and lack of enough focus on interpretability and clinical integration. These limitations drive the need for unified, computationally-efficient frameworks which are modality- and institution-agnostic and still robust in the face of limited supervision - the inspiration for the present study.
Materials and methods
Overview of the proposed framework

The proposed model functions to segment cartilage and bone structures within knee magnetic resonance imaging data using automated methods that lack manual annotation data. The method introduces an unsupervised segmentation approach that uses three major elements: it extracts features through contrastive learning and applies graph-based affinity propagation clustering and includes a temporal consistency system to maintain coherent and smooth segmentations between neighbouring MRI images. This integrative model design enables the system to discover distinct anatomical characteristics while dividing MRI voxels into intentional divisions and securing uniformity between adjacent MRI slices which enhances segmentation stability end to end MRI protocols. The proposed KneeSeg-U framework contains three main elements that overcome current challenges through the combination of self-supervised learning with modified U-Net architecture and feature consistency constraints and adversarial training approaches. The depiction of this framework appears in Fig. 1.
Overview of KneeSeg-U framework

KneeSeg-U makes use of medical-specific data transformation techniques together with contrastive learning to develop discriminative visual features that deliver exact segmentation results without relying on expert annotations. The system shows adaptability for various MRI protocols combined with anatomical variations which provides generalized performance.
The detailed schematic of the KneeSeg-U framework is given in Fig. 2.
Detailed schematic of the KneeSeg-U framework

The proposed framework starts with an unsupervised deep learning model that performs contrastive learning-based feature extraction on MRI scan images to extract valuable structural representations of knee cartilage and bones despite unlabeled data. An objective function in this model pushes dissimilar tissue types apart and keeps near together anatomical features with shared properties. The improved capability of future clustering procedures emerges from this process, which strengthens their discrimination of tissue types, especially when annotation labels are unavailable.

Following feature extraction, graph-based affinity propagation clustering is utilized to segment MRI patches into cartilage, bone, and background regions. Unlike traditional clustering techniques such as k-means or Gaussian mixture models (GMM), affinity propagation constructs a similarity graph that captures both voxel intensity and spatial information. This enables robust segmentation by ensuring that local anatomical structures are preserved during clustering. Furthermore, by eliminating the need for manually predefined cluster centroids, affinity propagation provides greater flexibility and adaptability to variations in MRI acquisition settings.

To further enhance segmentation consistency, particularly across MRI slices, the framework incorporates a temporal consistency module based on a multi-scale Gaussian process model. This module compensates for variable slice thicknesses (ranging from 0.5 mm to 3.0 mm) and mitigates segmentation discontinuities by enforcing anatomical coherence in the axial direction. By dynamically weighting slice-wise predictions based on acquisition parameters and local tissue continuity, the module effectively reduces segmentation artifacts and ensures smooth delineation of cartilage and bone structures. There are no explicit semantic labels in the proposed unsupervised framework in which the AI system gets trained. Instead, anatomically meaningful clusters corresponding to bone and cartilage emerge as a result of the combined effects of contrastive feature learning, spatially constrained affinity propagation clustering, and slice-wise consistency regularization.

It is important to note that there are usually areas of background signal in the knee MRI images that have similar signal intensity and texture properties as the bone or cartilage. The proposed unsupervised framework has explicitly dealt with this ambiguity, as it does not use the intensity information by itself. Instead, anatomically meaningful segmentation arises due to the cumulative effects of contrastive feature learning, spatially restricted graph-based affinity propagation clustering, along enforcing temporal consistency across slices. Contrastive learning tracks alterations to the feature embeddings at higher-order context and structure levels, and both the clustering and temporal modules add robust anatomical and spatial inductive biases. Together, these components are able to achieve reliable osteochondral segmentation between actual bone and cartilage structures and visually similar regions of background, in a completely unsupervised way.

Although the volumetric consistency is very important for the segmentation of knee MRI, the proposed framework is built upon a 2D network structure instead of a 3D network model. This choice of design is motivated by the heterogeneous nature of MRI acquisitions of the knee, which tend to have different slice thickness and inter-slice spacing in different subjects and on different scanners. A 2D architecture allows giving more robustness to such anisotropic data, while the volumetric coherence is enforced by a separate temporal coherence module.

The encoder of Modified U-Net is based on ResNet-50 as hierarchical feature extraction and the modified U-Net decoder has a standard U-Net style with attention guided skip connections. First, the feature encoder is used to extract dense representations of each pixel from each input image by using the Modified U-Net backbone. These feature maps represent their latent space that is optimized with both contrastive and clustering objectives in a joint manner. The embedded extracted embeddings are then sent to the clustering module in which pixels are grouped into anatomically meaningful regions using affinity-based clustering. The clustering assignments are used to generate pseudo-labels which are used to guide segmentation learning without manual annotations.

To force the coherence in space and time, the temporal consistency module works on consecutive image slices (or neighbouring frames) by encouraging consistent cluster assignment in neighbouring inputs. This module adds a regularization loss which penalizes the abrupt changes in the cluster labels in adjacent slices which helps to stabilize the segmentation boundaries and to reduce the slice-wise fragmentation. Again, in the training process, the gradients of the contrastive loss, clustering loss and the temporal consistency loss are jointly backpropagated to update the common feature encoder. This joint optimization allows the encoder to learn representations simultaneously discriminative, cluster-friendly and temporally stable and eventually leads to coherent and accurate segmentation results. All components of the proposed framework are trained in a fully unsupervised manner without access to ground-truth labels. Hyperparameters were selected using unlabeled validation data based on unsupervised optimization criteria. Expert annotations were employed only for quantitative evaluation and clinical assessment and were not used during training or model selection.

The proposed framework is evaluated on the Osteoarthritis Initiative (OAI) dataset, a large-scale repository of knee MRI scans widely used for osteoarthritis research. Performance is assessed using standard segmentation metrics, including the Dice similarity coefficient (DSC), Hausdorff distance, and volumetric consistency, with comparative analysis against existing state-of-the-art unsupervised segmentation methods. The experimental results demonstrate the efficacy of the proposed approach, achieving Dice similarity coefficients of 0.89 ± 0.03 for cartilage segmentation and 0.92 ± 0.02 for bone segmentation, significantly outperforming conventional unsupervised methods.
Modified U-Net architecture

Figure 3 shows the architecture of the proposed Modified U-Net architecture for accurate segmentation of medical images. The network is an encoder-decoder architecture, that is, containing a series of hierarchical feature extraction modules in the encoder with a decreased size, which are composed of multi-scale convolutional blocks and down-sampling operations.
Architecture of the proposed modified U-Net. encoder-decoder paths, attention-guided skip connections, and dilated residual bottleneck

Each encoder stage uses parallel convolutions to obtain both local and contextual information, and feature dimensionality is increased with depth. At the heart of the network, a dilated residual bottleneck is used to increase the receptive field and improve the contextual representation without the need to increase the spatial resolution. The decoder is used to reconstruct segmentation maps with high resolution using transposed convolution and combines encoder features using attention-guided skip connections, which selectively highlight anatomically-related areas and remove background noise. Finally, 1*1 convolution and sigmoid or SoftMax activation is applied to get the output segmentation mask. This design provides the advantage of being able to properly delineate boundaries for accurate segmentation, preserving the anatomical structure of thin structures, and strong segmentation performance in different imaging conditions.
Feature extraction using contrastive learning

Effective feature extraction is critical for unsupervised segmentation, as it determines the quality of subsequent clustering and segmentation performance. In this study, we employ contrastive learning to extract discriminative representations from knee MRI scans, enabling the model to learn meaningful anatomical features without requiring manually labeled data. The contrastive learning framework ensures that feature embeddings of anatomically similar voxels are pulled closer, while those of distinct tissue types (cartilage, bone, background) are pushed apart in the latent space. This approach enhances the separability of different tissue structures, facilitating improved segmentation performance in downstream clustering. Before feature extraction, MRI scans undergo a standardized preprocessing pipeline to improve data consistency and reduce artifacts, bone, and background regions. The preprocessing steps are illustrated in Fig. 4, it mainly includes:

A non-local means denoising filter is applied to suppress high-frequency noise while preserving tissue boundaries.

The study employs a self-supervised contrastive learning framework to learn robust feature representations from the pre-processed MRI scans. Our approach follows a SimCLR-based contrastive loss formulation adapted for medical imaging. To create positive and negative sample pairs for contrastive learning, we apply domain-specific augmentation strategies. Two augmentations of the same MRI patch (e.g., intensity shifts, local deformations, and small affine transformations) form a positive pair. Patches from different anatomical locations within the same scan or from different subjects serve as negative samples.

Given an MRI patch , its augmented counterpart  is treated as a positive sample, while all other patches in the batch serve as negative samples . The contrastive learning model is trained using Normalized Temperature-scaled Cross-Entropy Loss, defined by the Eq. (2)

τ is a temperature scaling parameter that controls the separation margin between positive and negative samples. This objective function maximizes agreement between positive pairs while minimizing similarity between negative pairs, thereby ensuring that learned representations effectively distinguish cartilage.

The contrastive learning part of KneeSeg-U was implemented with a batch size of 256. For each anchor, 1–2 positive samples were produced using standard data augmentation, whereas implicit negatives were taken from other samples in the same batch of samples. There was no explicit hard negative mining used. The temperature parameter (t) in the contrastive loss was set to 0.07 which controls the scaling of similarities of positive and negative pairs. These settings in combination with affinity propagation clustering and temporal consistency regularization help the model to learn robust and anatomically meaningful feature representations. Importantly, all the hyperparameters were chosen using only unlabeled MRI Volumes, with the model convergence being monitored by the presence of intrinsic unsupervised objectives such as compactness of features, clustering stability, and temporal consistency. This ensures the fact that KneeSeg-U is strictly fully unsupervised, there is no direct or indirect access to expert annotations during the training or validation process.
Emergence of anatomically meaningful clusters in an unsupervised setting

Contrastive learning within the proposed framework is used for the learning of a discriminative latent embedding space instead of a direct assignment of semantic tissue label. By performing the two tasks of maximizing the agreement between augmented views of the same voxel and separating dissimilar samples, the model causes voxels with similar anatomical and imaging characteristics to form compact and well-separated manifolds in feature space. In the MRI of the knee, the bone, cartilage and background have different intensity distribution, texture pattern and structural continuity. Through intensive perturbations in the forms of the domain specific augmentations i.e. intensity perturbations, elastic deformations, and affine transformations, the contrastive objective captures the augmentation-invariant features preserving the intrinsic tissue identities. As a result, the learned embeddings are stable between slices and subjects, and it is a good foundation for further unsupervised clustering, but contrastive learning is not a necessary but not sufficient condition for semantic segmentation.

The semantic correspondence of the clusters with the bone and cartilage is seen during the graph-based affinity propagation clustering stage that considers the feature similarity and combines it with spatial proximity and local anatomic coherence. Bone structures have high spatial continuity and relative homogenous signal intensity, and well separated with cartilage and other organs and tissues. The spatially weighted affinity formulation is therefore encouraging a formation of contiguous features (consistent in features) and avoids a situation where bone voxels are broken across the clusters or fused with the neighbour tissues. This anatomical coherence is also enforced by the temporal coherence module which penalizes incoherencies slice-by-slice and ensures the smooth 3D coherence in the axial direction. Finally, with use of objective anatomical information such as intensity statistics, location and extent in volume without manual annotation, cluster-to-anatomy attribution is performed post hoc. Together, all these pieces help to naively get anatomically meaningful segmentation to work reliably.
Network architecture for feature extraction

ResNet-50 backbone with domain-specific modifications for MRI feature extraction is employed in this study. The first convolutional layer is adjusted to accommodate single-channel grayscale MRI input. A projection head (two-layer MLP) maps high-dimensional features to a 128-dimensional embedding space, where contrastive learning is performed. The output feature embeddings are then passed to the clustering module for segmentation. Specifically, the projection head is a two-layer multilayer perceptron (MLP) network, which has a hidden layer with a dimension of 512 and the output embedding layer with a dimension of 128, using ReLU as the activation function and batch normalization is used between the layers. During training, contrastive pairs are created by anatomically plausible augmentations, which consist of random rotations (+-15 deg), scaling (0.9–1.1), intensity jittering and Gaussian noise injection and elastic deformation to guarantee the invariance to acquisition variability and maintain structural semantics.

For each mini-batch, positive samples are created by applying two different augmentations on the same input image independently and negative samples are retrieved from other images contained in the batch. A temperature-scaled InfoNCE (Information Noise Contrastive Estimation) loss is used for maximizing agreement of positive pairs and separation from negative pairs. These additions improve the understanding of learning of discriminative and invariant feature representations in the proposed framework and increase the methodological transparency of the study.
Output feature representations

After training, each MRI voxel is assigned a feature vector in the learned embedding space. These feature vectors serve as input to the graph-based affinity propagation clustering module, where segmentation into cartilage, bone, and background regions is performed. The contrastive learning framework ensures that these feature representations are discriminative, domain-adaptive, and generalizable across different MRI acquisition protocols. The features are illustrated using UMAP plot and t-SNE plot in Fig. 5. The learned features provide a robust foundation for the subsequent clustering-based segmentation approach, described in the next section.
Feature visualization
Graph-based affinity propagation clustering

Graph affinity propagation clustering is used in this study to segment knee MRI voxels into cartilage, bone, and background regions. Unlike k-means or Gaussian mixture models (GMM), affinity propagation does not require a predefined number of clusters and dynamically selects exemplars through an iterative message-passing process. This makes it particularly suited for medical imaging, where anatomical structures vary across subjects, and spatial coherence is crucial. While k-means relies purely on intensity-based similarity and GMM assumes Gaussian distributions, affinity propagation integrates both feature-based and spatial information, ensuring anatomically meaningful segmentation.

The algorithm is driven by an affinity matrix that encodes the similarity between voxel feature embeddings. We compute similarity using a combination of cosine similarity between learned representations and a spatial constraint to enforce anatomical consistency. Given feature embeddings extracted via contrastive learning, the cosine similarity between voxels ​ and ​ is defined by the Eq. (4)
To further encourage local smoothness, we integrate a spatial weighting term and it is denoted by the Eq. (5)

where  is the Euclidean distance between voxels, and λ1, λ2 ​ balance feature similarity and spatial proximity. This ensures adjacent voxels with similar intensity and locations are more likely to belong to the same anatomical region.

Affinity propagation utilizes a message passing process to iteratively refine cluster assignments. Each voxel initially serves as a potential cluster center, with an assigned preference score determining its likelihood of being an exemplar. Two messages are exchanged iteratively: responsibility, which reflects how well a voxel, serves as an exemplar for another, and availability, which indicates how likely a voxel is to be an exemplar based on feedback from other voxels. These updates converge when cluster assignments stabilize, typically within 100–200 iterations. After convergence, each voxel is assigned to the exemplar that maximizes the combined availability and responsibility and it is given by the Eq. (6)

The result is a segmentation where cartilage, bone, and background regions are consistently delineated across MRI scans. To improve efficiency, similarity calculations are restricted to local neighbourhoods, reducing memory overhead, and GPU acceleration is employed to ensure rapid convergence. This method balances segmentation accuracy and computational feasibility, making it suitable for large-scale MRI datasets.

Although some areas of the background may have MR signal intensities that match complex structures, they are rarely spatially continuous, geometrically regular, or anatomically coherent with osseous structures. The proposed affinity propagation clustering takes these different characteristics into explicit consideration by combining the similarity of features with the constraints of spatial proximity. The spatial weighting term penalizes regions that are isolated or fragmented and appear to be bone only because of their intensity, but do not form anatomically plausible and contiguous structures. As a result, background voxels with bone-like appearance are not likely to form stable exemplars in the message-passing process, while actual bone regions (graphic and consistent morphology) will form dominant and coherent clusters.

For affinity propagation clustering, the maximum number of iterations was set as 200 and damping factor was set as 0.9 to get stable convergence. Stable cluster assignments were defined as the convergence criterion as being defined in 15 successive iterations. Similarity between voxel embeddings, obtains similarity between learned features using the combination of cosine similarity and the use of a spatial weighting term which enforces the anatomical coherence. For this reason, this is a good formulation to make sure that neighboring areas of bone, cartilage, and background are well delineated and spurious clusters are not created by isolated or fragmented voxels.
Temporal consistency module for slice-wise smoothing

Instead of directly incorporating in 3D network dependencies in the volumetric space, in the proposed approach, slice-wise temporal consistency as a post-processing regularization step is introduced. Fully 3D unsupervised models are computationally expensive and suffer from acquisition heterogeneity as well as manual annotation shortages. The temporal consistency module offers a flexible and interpretable approach to ensure the anatomical consistency between slices with stability and efficiency of 2D contrastive feature learning.

Segmentation of knee MRI scans requires consistency across slices to ensure anatomical coherence, particularly given the variable slice thickness (0.5–3.0 mm) in different acquisitions. Without enforcing smooth transitions between adjacent slices, segmentation results may appear fragmented, leading to inconsistencies in 3D reconstructions. To address this, we incorporate a temporal consistency module that enforces smooth segmentation boundaries while preserving fine anatomical details.

This module leverages a multi-scale Gaussian process model to adaptively weight slice-wise predictions. Given a set of voxel-wise segmentations  for slice , the refined segmentation  is obtained by considering predictions from neighboring slices as given the Eq. (7)
where ​ represents neighbouring slices and ​ is an adaptive weight based on spatial proximity and segmentation confidence. These weights are determined using a Gaussian kernel by the Eq. (8)
where the first term penalizes large inter-slice distances and the second term incorporates segmentation similarity , computed as the Dice coefficient between segmentations of adjacent slices.
To further refine boundary continuity, we impose an edge-preserving constraint using total variation regularization is given by the Eq. (9)

where  represents the spatial gradient of segmentation labels, ensuring that boundaries remain smooth without excessive blurring. Through the temporal consistency module implementation with spatial weighting and segmentation similarity, together with boundary-preserving constraints, we can achieve better reduction of artifacts between MRI slices. The technique employs quick sparse matrix processing and delivers computational efficiency with accurate MRI slice representation. To guarantee the transparency of parameter selection, the scale of Gaussian kernel s and segmentation similarity weight b are adaptive to determine. The parameter s is to control the effect of neighbouring slices in the temporal smoothing. In order to correct for variable slice thickness of the MRI acquisition, which varies from 0.5 to 3.0 mm, s is set up within 0.5–1.5 in which neighboring slices of the image have more impact and the farther slices have less impact (maintaining anatomical coherence yet keeping details of fine structure from being over-smoothing) The parameter b modulates the contribution of similarity of segmentation results between slices, obtained by the use of the Dice coefficient. It is tuned by an unsupervised validation procedure which is so inherent that it balances smoothness and structural fidelity. To suppress the effect of spurious predictions on inconsistent regions b are larger values whereas to maintain the genuine anatomical variations b are smaller values. Together with these parameters, the temporal consistency module can facilitate that the model enforces these slice consistency parameters, smoothness and boundary preservation while absorbing fragmentation and artifacts while preserving fine anatomical structures in an unsupervised training framework. The effects of the temporal consistency module are illustrated in Fig. 6.
Effects of temporal consistency module

The temporal consistency module further alleviates the temporal coherence along the slice-wise direction. Multi-scale Gaussian weighting is used over the adjacent slices and an edge preserving total variation constraint preserves the boundary fidelity and helps in reducing the artifacts. These parameters paired with the contrastive learning are used to generate strong, anatomically meaningful segmentation without the need for any expert annotations night maintaining an unsupervised training pipeline.

The temporal consistency module is another technique to further degrade the false positive segmentation of background regions by enforcing the slice-wise as well as three-dimensional anatomical coherence. Although background structures can seem intermittently like bone or cartilage between slices, usually they do not have the same consistent appearance and continuity as a whole structural entity between slices. By adaptively weighing nearby slice predictions and penalizing the inconsistency of orthogonal slices, the temporal consistency module provides spurious regions, suppresses spurious regions while reinforcing anatomically-valid bone and cartilage structures which remain over the imaging volume. This slice-wise regularization is specifically good in decreasing fragmentation and enhancing robustness in the presence of intensity ambiguities.
Adversarial consistency learning module

The proposed KneeSeg-U introduces an adversarial consistency learning strategy for robust and generalizable learning of the learned representations for heterogeneous MRI acquisitions. It is important to clear that this mechanism is not a normal Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) when the generator and discriminator have to compete explicitly with each other. Instead, adversarial learning is set up as a regularization learning that enforces learned feature invariance under perturbations and domain variations in the unsupervised learning setting.

The proposed formulation the Modified U-Net backbone is used as the feature encoder G(.), which is used to map an input MRI slice x into a latent representation z = G(x), which is then mapped into segmentation predictions S(x). To enhance the robustness, adversarial perturbations are added at the input and features with the help of stochastic transformations like intensity shifts, elastic deformations and spatial augmentations. The objective is to make sure that the model generates the same results for perturbed versions of the same input. The framework is composed of a modified version of the U-Net network, contrastive feature learning, anatomically guided affinity propagation clustering and slice-wise temporal consistency in one single end-to-end unsupervised segmentation pipeline. The training goals are explicitly defined as an optimization problem which is cooperative by taking into account (1) the contrastive loss to enhance the discriminative and anatomically meaningful of features, (2) the clustering loss to direct the formation of coherent regions for the bone, the cartilage and the background in images, (3) the temporal consistency loss to enforce the smoothness and the volumetric coherence of the observations across the slices. These objectives are backpropagated simultaneously to update the shared feature encoder so that it ensures the whole part, network architecture, representation learning, and post processing regularization, have a coherent contribution to anatomically accurate segmentation.

Methodological distinction from existing unsupervised frameworks

The proposed KneeSeg-U framework is unique not by the individual use of contrastive learning, affinity propagation clustering or temporal consistency, but by their anatomically guided and closely intertwined use in a unified unsupervised end-to-end segmentation pipeline. Unlike previous works which mainly deal with representation learning or generic clustering methods, KneeSeg-U presents anatomically informed affinity propagation clustering which takes into account feature similarity and spatial proximity jointly to ensure the segmentation of contiguous and anatomically meaningful regions of cartilage, bone and background. In addition, the slice aware temporal consistency module is also dynamically adapted to different MRI slice thickness for applying coherent segmentations among the adjacent slices while maintaining small anatomical details. The adversarial optimized Modified U-Net architecture further retains the continuity of structures, with the attention guided skip connections and the residual bottlenecks to improve anatomical continuity. Together, these integrated components enable KneeSeg-U for annotation-free segmentation with a capability for robust segmentation contrasting the heterogeneity of MRI acquisition, anatomical variability as well as background ambiguities.
Experimental results and analysis

This section presents a comprehensive evaluation of the proposed KneeSeg-U framework for unsupervised cartilage and bone segmentation in knee MRI. The datasets used for evaluation, are described first, followed by implementation details and evaluation metrics. Then presents the quantitative comparisons with state-of-the-art methods, qualitative segmentation results, ablation studies to analyze the contribution of each component, and finally, a discussion of clinical applicability and limitations.
Datasets
The KneeSeg-U is evaluated on three publicly available knee MRI datasets to ensure comprehensive assessment across diverse acquisition protocols and patient populations:

Osteoarthritis Initiative (OAI): utilized 300 3D DESS (Double Echo Steady State) MRI scans with a resolution of 0.37 × 0.37 × 0.7 mm³. These scans represent varying stages of osteoarthritis, from healthy knees to severe disease.

SKI10 Dataset: This dataset consists of 100 knee MRI volumes acquired from multiple clinical centers with heterogeneous scanning parameters, making it ideal for evaluating generalization capabilities68. The resolution ranges from 0.4 × 0.4 × 1.0 mm³ to 0.5 × 0.5 × 1.5 mm³.

FastMRI Knee Dataset: We selected 150 knee MRI scans from the FastMRI dataset, featuring different pulse sequences (T1-weighted, T2-weighted, and PD-weighted) with resolutions varying from 0.4 × 0.4 × 3.0 mm³ to 0.5 × 0.5 × 3.5 mm³.

For validation purposes, the expert manual segmentations provided with these datasets are utilized. Importantly, these annotations were used only for evaluation and not during the training process, maintaining the unsupervised nature of our approach. All the datasets used in this study are available to the public and have been anonymised entirely in compliance with ethical guidelines. No personally identifiable information was accessed or used at any stage of the study. The Osteoarthritis Initiative (OAI), SKI10, and FastMRI Knee datasets have been collected under the approval of the institutional review boards (IRBs), and the use of these datasets for research purposes is appropriate according to the data-sharing and ethical regulations. Researchers can obtain these data sets directly from the respective datasets repository to ensure the transparency and the reproducibility of the reported results.

In order to have a consistent outcome from MRI scans from various scanners, protocols and institutions, all images went through a standard preprocessing pipeline. First, N4 bias field correction was used to eliminate low frequency intensity non-uniformities. Next, z-score normalization was done per volume, within which the mean and standard deviation of voxel intensities were subtracted and divided respectively, in order to standardize the distribution of intensities. Finally, all voxel intensities were rescaled within a fixed range [0, 1] in order to harmonize the contrasts from dataset to dataset and MRI sequence. This cross-dataset normalization guarantees that the proposed KneeSeg-U framework could learn meaningful feature representations whatever screen scanner or protocol-intosephot is delayed and experienced, in order to improve the generalization in multi-center research.
Implementation details

The KneeSeg-U framework was implemented using PyTorch 1.9.0 and trained on an NVIDIA A100 GPU with 40GB memory. The contrastive learning model was trained for 500 epochs using the Adam optimizer with a learning rate of 1e-4 and a batch size of 256. Temperature parameter τ in the contrastive loss was set to 0.07. For the affinity propagation clustering, parameters λ₁ and λ₂ (balancing feature similarity and spatial proximity) were set to 0.7 and 0.3, respectively, determined through grid search on a small validation subset. The temporal consistency module used a Gaussian kernel with adaptive σ based on slice thickness, typically ranging from 0.5 to 1.5.

For all datasets (OAI, SKI10 and FastMRI Knee), volumes of MR images were separated at the subject level, to avoid data leaking. In particular, 70% of the subjects were used for unsupervised training on contrastive learning, affinity clustering, and temporal consistency without access to any expert annotations. An additional 10% of subjects were left for unlabeled validation which was used only for monitoring the convergence and adjusting hyperparameters according to intrinsic unsupervised objectives, such as clustering stability, feature embedding compactness and temporal consistency. The remaining 20% of subjects were kept out as the test set, in which segmentation performance was tested on expert-provided annotations. These annotations were never used in the training process or validation process in order to maintain the fully unsupervised nature of KneeSeg-U.

Performance evaluation
The study employs the following metrics to evaluate segmentation performance:
Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC): Measures spatial overlap between predicted and ground truth segmentations, ranging from 0 (no overlap) to 1 (perfect overlap).
Average Surface Distance (ASD): Calculates the average distance between segmentation boundaries, measured in millimeters.
Hausdorff Distance (HD): Represents the maximum distance between segmentation boundaries, providing insight into the worst-case segmentation error.
Volumetric Similarity (VS): Quantifies the similarity between segmented volumes, indicating the clinical relevance of the segmentation.
Computational Efficiency: Measured as the average processing time per scan in seconds.

The results demonstrate that KneeSeg-U achieves segmentation performance comparable to supervised methods and significantly outperforms existing unsupervised approaches. For cartilage segmentation, the proposed KneeSeg-U method attains a Dice similarity coefficient of 0.87. Similarly, for bone segmentation, KneeSeg-U achieves a DSC of 0.91, approaching the performance of self supervised learning (0.93) and exceeding all other methods.

The average surface distance (ASD) and Hausdorff distance (HD) metrics further confirm the superior boundary delineation capabilities of our approach. Notably, KneeSeg-U achieves an ASD of 0.47 mm for cartilage and 0.44 mm for bone, indicating high precision in boundary localization. The volumetric similarity scores of 94.3% for cartilage and 95.7% for bone demonstrate the clinical relevance of our segmentations, as volumetric measurements are crucial for monitoring disease progression in osteoarthritis.

To further demonstrate the capability of KneeSeg-U to learn discriminative feature representations and anatomically meaningful feature representations, t-SNE the embeddings learned by KneeSeg-U for one representative MRI volume is demonstrated as shown in Fig. 9. The resulting clustering of the cartilage, bone, and background results has a clear separability, which indicates the effectiveness of the combination of contrastive learning and affinity clustering and temporal consistency in encoding structural information.
t-SNE visualization of KneeSeg-U learned feature embeddings from a representative knee MRI volume

The t-SNE plot shows a two-dimensional projection of high-dimensional feature embeddings which are extracted by KneeSeg-U. Each of the points is a feature of the voxels that are color coded according to the tissue type (cartilage, bone, background). The fact that clusters are well separated is an indication KneeSeg-U well learns discriminative features representing anatomical structures without any manual annotations. Cartilage and bone embeddings form well-separated, tight clusters and the background voxels are grouped in a separate way, which reflects the ability of this model to retain tissue-specific representations. This visualization compliments quantitative result, and offers an intuitive evidence for the capability of the framework to maintain the anatomical consistency and for the accurate segmentation. Case-level performance analysis and failure study

The proposed KneeSeg-U was judged on a case-by-case basis using the Osteoarthritis Initiative (OAI) dataset. The mean, standard deviation (SD), and worst-case values of the DSC are reported for all volumes for both cartilage and bone segmentation in Table 15. On average, the segmentation of cartilage allowed a DSC of 0.89+-0.03 and bone segmentation a DSC of 0.92+-0.02. The worst-case DSC per volume was 0.81 for cartilage and 0.87 for bone indicating challenging instances. A detailed failure-case analysis showed that there are common sources of segmentation errors:
Severe cartilage thinning: Thin or degenerated regions of cartilage resulted in under-segmentation, as a result of low voxel contrast and partial volume effect.
Motion artifacts Patient movement during scanning created inconsistencies of intensities among the slices resulting in fragmented or misaligned segments.
Low-contrast slices: Sometimes the low signal to noise ratio of a specific MRI slice or the tissue contrast led to a boundary ambiguity, especially in the junction between the cartilage and bone.

These cases show the limitations of the framework in cases of extreme anatomical variation or presence of imaging artifacts, and give us an idea on how to improve our framework in the future, such as adding multi-modal MRI inputs, or to make it more robust to training noises. The case level performance statistics of the proposed KneeSeg-U framework, on the knee MRI dataset, are presented in Table 15. The results are shown for the mean, standard deviation (SD), and worst case of DSC over volume for the segmentation of cartilage and bone. These statistics give an in-depth assessment of segmentation consistency among individual MRI scans, both in terms of the average performance and of challenging cases.

The results presented in Table 15 show high and stable segmentation performance, independent of volume. Cartilage segmentation resulted in a mean DSC of 0.89 and a standard deviation of 0.03, indicating that the results are consistent with little variability between cases. The worst-case DSC of 0.81 indicates that even though performance is good overall, there are certain volumes which present more challenges (especially if they have severe degeneration or imaging artifacts). Bone segmentation demonstrates even greater robustness, the average DSC of the segmentation being 0.92 with lower variability (SD = 0.02), due to rather homogenous intensity and structural continuity of the bone tissue. The worst-case bone DSC of 0.87 gives further confirmation for the reliability of the framework. Overall, the case-level analysis is useful in validating the stability and generalisability of KneeSeg-U, as well as provided areas for targeted improvement.

To increase the clinical relevance of the proposed KneeSeg-U framework, a structured expert-based evaluation was performed by 2 board-certified musculoskeletal radiologists with more than 8 and 12 years of clinical experience, respectively. The evaluation was done based on clinically meaningful segmentation quality instead of assigning purely algorithmic measures. A structured radiologist-based evaluation was performed based on five predefined clinical criteria, such as anatomical correctness, the accuracy of boundaries at the osteochondral interface, the preservation of thin cartilage areas, false positive suppression, and the clinical usability.

Each criterion was rated on a five-point Likert scale. KneeSeg-U was rated with a mean clinical reliability score of 4.6, with good inter-rater agreement (k = 0.82), indicating that it can be used clinically. Table 17 shows the radiologist-based clinical reliability comparison of supervised and unsupervised methods of knee MRI segmentation. Segmentations were separately reviewed by two board-certified musculoskeletal radiologists using five clinically relevant criteria that were scored on a five-point Likert scale.

Table 17 shows a qualitative clinical comparison of Knee cartilage segmentation methods on a five point evaluation scale. The KneeSeg-U method proposed here shows consistently high performance across all the criteria, such as anatomical correctness, boundary accuracy, thin cartilage preservation and false positive suppression that represent good clinical robustness. Semi-supervised and patch attention-based methods exhibit moderate levels of reliability, and diffusion- and transformer-based ones exhibit comparatively low reliability, especially in retaining thin cartilage structures. In all, this KneeSeg-U offers an all-around well-balanced clinically reliable solution that can be deployed for routine orthopedic imaging use-flows.
Qualitative visualizations

To accompany quantitative evaluation, qualitative evaluations of the KneeSeg-U framework are provided in Figs. 10, 11 and 12. Multi-slice overlays, boundary zoom-ins and 3D reconstructions provide insight into the ability to delineate accurate cartilage and bone with continuity of anatomy and volume consistency to provide robust evidence of effectiveness of the model for generation of high-fidelity unsupervised segmentation of knee MRI segments across different imaging conditions.

Boundary zoom-in comparisons highlighting fine anatomical delineation

The segmentation results of cartilage and bone structures of several consecutive knee MRI slices are shown in Fig. 10. The overlays show how the proposed KneeSeg-U framework maintains the continuity of anatomical structures and details about the structure in the axial dimension. Cartilage and bone areas are clearly distinguished from the background, underscoring the model to suffer from the ability to resolve the boundary between fine tissues without the help of manual annotations. They are used as a qualitative complement to the quantitative evaluation metrics and validate the work of the unsupervised segmentation approach.

Figure 11 shows more detail in zoom-in views of the cartilage and bone boundaries to highlight the precision of the proposed segmentation framework. The comparisons can be seen to depict how the KneeSeg-U model is able to accurately delineate thin structures of anatomy, even in regions with complex morphology or low contrast. Fine-grained structural elements, such as edges of cartilage next to bones and surfaces of joints, are well preserved without any spurious fragmentation. These visualizations focusing on boundaries are important for validating the effectiveness of the framework to preserve anatomically fidelity and it complements global segmentation metrics. By delivering localised measurements of the accuracy of the structure, It shows the ability to obtain anatomically meaningful segmentation at high resolution for a range of MRI acquisition protocols with the framework.
Discussions

The proposed KneeSeg-U framework shows that precise cartilage and bone segmentation in the knee MRI can be performed in a fully unsupervised manner without any manual annotation for training and model selection. By combining contrast features learning, graph-based affinity propagation clustering and temporal consistency regularization, the framework can successfully capture the anatomical structure, suppress the ambiguities of intensity and enforce the slice-wise coherence. Quantitative and qualitative assessments on multiple datasets demonstrate that KneeSeg-U reliably surpasses existing unsupervised algorithms and approaches that of supervised ones, although KneeSeg-U offers great generalization capability across heterogeneous Multiple MRI protocols.

The framework is grounded in a 2D architecture, with volumetric coherence being enforced by post-processing instead of the direct 3D modeling which might pose a limitation to capture long-range spatial context. Capturing long range spatial context is inherently limited as compared to fully 3D network and the computational overhead is increased by the affinity propagation clustering step. Post hoc cluster to anatomy attribution may be influenced by extreme variations of anatomy or rare pathologies. In addition, segmentation performance can be adversely affected in knees with severe osteoarthritis where the cartilage is very thin or absent and in cases with motion artifacts, slice misalignment or metallic implants. Despite the effects of preprocessing, such as intensity normalization and bias correction may have certain subtle effects of the scanner specific variation, which may affect the feature embeddings, especially for multi-center data-sets. Furthermore, the framework might not generalize well to rare pathologies of the knee such as tumors, severe ligament injuries or post-surgical alterations. Overall, the 2D slice-wise approach with temporal consistency offers a computationally efficient alternative with strong volumetric coherence but these factors outline areas for future improvement, in terms of dealing with extreme anatomical and imaging variability.

From the practical point of view, KneeSeg-U has great clinical and research value. Its annotation free design allows it to be easily deployed to different institutions and scanners allowing decreased reliance on expert labelling. The accuracy and strength demonstrated and the rapidly derivable inference is useful for large-scale osteoarthritis research, longitudinal analysis and clinical decision making that enable reliable and reproducible segmentation of knee MRI in instance settings.
Conclusion and future directions

This study introduced KneeSeg-U, a novel unsupervised deep learning framework for automatic cartilage and bone segmentation in knee MRI that eliminates the dependency on manually annotated datasets while maintaining high segmentation accuracy. By integrating contrastive learning-based feature extraction, graph-based affinity propagation clustering, and a temporal consistency module, our framework successfully addresses the critical limitations of existing knee MRI segmentation approaches. Experimental results demonstrate that KneeSeg-U achieves segmentation performance comparable to supervised methods (Dice similarity coefficients of 0.87 for cartilage and 0.91 for bone) while significantly outperforming conventional unsupervised techniques across diverse MRI protocols and datasets. The framework provides a clinically scalable solution that reduces dependency on expert annotations, enabling more efficient and widespread application of automated knee MRI analysis for early musculoskeletal disorder detection and monitoring.

Future work is aimed at pushing the limit of the KneeSeg-U framework to other musculoskeletal joints, such as hip, shoulder, ankle, etc. Musculoskeletal joints have unique anatomical complexities and variable cartilage geometries. Enhancements of feature representation learning in alternative self-supervised paradigms will be investigated in order to capture such joint specific patterns better. The framework will also be adapted to detect pathological features, like cartilage lesions and bone marrow edema, in a totally unsupervised way, and for longitudinal analysis to detect structural changes over time. Further development may involve multi modal integration of complementary imaging sequence, the incorporation of explainable techniques of AI that can improve the clinical interpretability of AI, and federated learning approaches for collaborative model improvement between institutions without sharing sensitive patient data. These directions are aimed to promote the development of the field of annotation free medical image segmentation and provide a method for developing fully automated and clinically robust analysis in the field of medical imaging in various musculoskeletal applications.
